questions.question:
Given below are two statements followed by two conclusions. Assuming these statements to be true, decide which one logically follows.

Statements:
I. No manager is a leader.
II. All leaders are executives.

Conclusions:
I. No manager is an executive.
II. No executive is a manager.

A. Only conclusion I follows.
B. Only conclusion II follows.
C. Neither conclusion I nor II follows.
D. Both conclusions I and II follow.
questions.answer: C. Neither conclusion I nor II follows.
questions.to_find: which conclusion logically follows from the two given statements

understand.plain: We have two categorical statements: no manager is a leader, and all leaders are executives. We must determine whether either conclusion can be deduced strictly from these facts. The key insight is that the statements tell us nothing about whether managers can also be executives through some path other than being leaders.

keywords.explain: A deductive argument built from two premises and one conclusion, each of the form 'All A are B', 'No A is B', or 'Some A are/are not B'.
keywords.example: All dogs are mammals; all mammals breathe; therefore all dogs breathe.

keywords.explain: A term is distributed in a statement when the statement says something about every member of that category.
keywords.example: In 'No manager is a leader', both 'manager' and 'leader' are distributed.

keywords.explain: Concluding a relationship between two categories based on their separate, incomplete connections to a third category.
keywords.example: Inferring 'No manager is an executive' from 'No manager is a leader' and 'All leaders are executives' — the middle term 'leader' does not cover all executives.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing managers, leaders, and executives as partially overlapping sets.

given.aim: Determine which conclusion about managers and executives follows from the premises.

terms.term: Statement I: No manager is a leader
terms.meaning: The sets 'managers' and 'leaders' are completely disjoint.
terms.example: Manager M and leader L cannot be the same person.
terms.connects: Restricts the relationship between managers and leaders.

terms.term: Statement II: All leaders are executives
terms.meaning: The set 'leaders' is entirely contained within the set 'executives'.
terms.example: Every person who is a leader is also an executive.
terms.connects: Links leaders to executives but does not say anything about non-leader executives.

terms.term: Conclusion I: No manager is an executive
terms.meaning: Claims that the sets 'managers' and 'executives' are completely disjoint.
terms.example: A manager could still be an executive by a route other than being a leader.
terms.connects: This goes beyond what the premises state; executives include non-leaders.

given.plan: Test each conclusion by trying to construct a scenario consistent with both premises that falsifies the conclusion. If such a scenario exists, the conclusion does not follow.

steps.title: Map the premises as set relations
steps.apply: Statement I: manager(x) -> NOT leader(x). Statement II: leader(x) -> executive(x). The set of executives is larger than the set of leaders; it may also contain non-leaders.
steps.note: The premises do not exclude the possibility that some managers are executives without being leaders.

steps.title: Test Conclusion I: No manager is an executive
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
steps.apply: Suppose a person P is both a manager and an executive but NOT a leader. Statement I is satisfied (P is not a leader). Statement II is satisfied (it does not claim P). So Conclusion I fails — a manager CAN be an executive.
steps.note: Conclusion I incorrectly assumes executives = leaders, but executives can include non-leaders.

steps.title: Test Conclusion II: No executive is a manager
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: This is the converse of Conclusion I. The same counterexample defeats it: a person can be both an executive (through a non-leader path) and a manager. Conclusion II also fails.
steps.note: Neither conclusion can be established from the given premises.

solution.result: Neither Conclusion I nor Conclusion II logically follows from the given statements. Option C is correct.

questions.formula_note: No new formulas needed; the reasoning uses basic categorical logic and the contrapositive rule.

questions.question:
Fact 1: Humans are mammals.
Fact 2: Some humans are engineers.
Fact 3: Engineers build houses.

If the above statements are facts, which of the following can be logically inferred?
I. All mammals build houses.
II. Engineers are mammals.
III. Some humans are not engineers.

A. II only.
B. III only.
C. I and II.
D. I only.
questions.to_find: which of the three inferences can be logically deduced from the three given facts

understand.plain: Three facts establish relationships between humans, mammals, and engineers. We need to check which of three proposed inferences can be guaranteed by these facts alone. The critical observation is that 'Some humans are engineers' directly implies 'Some humans are NOT engineers' because a universal claim was not made.

keywords.explain: Claims every member of category A belongs to category B.
keywords.example: Fact 1: 'Humans are mammals' means every human is a mammal.

keywords.explain: Claims at least one but not necessarily all members of A belong to B.
keywords.example: Fact 2: 'Some humans are engineers' — not all humans are engineers.

keywords.explain: A statement that must be true given that the premises are true, with no additional assumptions.
keywords.example: From 'Some humans are engineers' alone we can infer 'Some humans are not engineers' only if we know not ALL humans are engineers.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: mammals contains humans; humans partially overlaps engineers; engineers build houses.

given.aim: Identify which inferences are guaranteed by the given facts.

terms.meaning: The set of humans is a subset of the set of mammals.
terms.example: Every human (engineer or not) is also a mammal.
terms.connects: Allows us to link engineers (a subset of humans) to mammals.

terms.meaning: There is at least one human who is an engineer, but not all humans are engineers.
terms.example: Since 'some' is used, there exist humans who are not engineers.
terms.connects: Provides the partial overlap between humans and engineers.

terms.meaning: The set of engineers is a subset of the set of house-builders.

given.plan: Evaluate each proposed inference against the three facts to see if it is necessarily true, possibly true, or provably false.

steps.title: Evaluate Inference I: All mammals build houses
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: We know some humans (who are mammals) are engineers who build houses. But mammals include non-human animals (dogs, cats, whales) about whom no building claim is made. Inference I is too broad and cannot be established. FALSE.
steps.note: Confuses 'some mammals (namely engineers among humans) build houses' with 'all mammals build houses'.

steps.title: Evaluate Inference II: Engineers are mammals
steps.formula_raw: 'All A are B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Fact 2 says 'Some humans are engineers'. This means engineers in question are human. Fact 1 says all humans are mammals. Therefore those engineers are mammals. BUT: the facts say nothing about engineers from non-human sources; however, we can only talk about the given universe. Still, Fact 2 only says SOME humans are engineers — it does not say ALL engineers are humans. Engineers from other species (hypothetically) might exist. Inference II cannot be guaranteed from the facts as stated. FALSE.
steps.note: Engineers being mammals requires all engineers to be human, which is not stated.

steps.title: Evaluate Inference III: Some humans are not engineers
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: Fact 2 states 'Some humans are engineers', using the word 'some' which in formal logic means 'at least one but not all' (under the standard GATE interpretation of exclusive-some or simply since 'All humans are engineers' is not given). The fact uses 'some', signalling a proper subset relationship. Therefore, there exist humans who are NOT engineers. Inference III is TRUE.
steps.note: In GATE verbal reasoning, 'Some A are B' implies 'Some A are not B' — it is not equivalent to 'All A are B'.

solution.result: Only Inference III (Some humans are not engineers) can be logically inferred. Option B is correct.

questions.formula_note: Standard categorical logic rules from the formula registry are sufficient; no new formulas required.

questions.question:
Fact: If it rains, then the field is wet.

Read the following statements:
i. It rains
ii. The field is not wet
iii. The field is wet
iv. It did not rain

Which one of the options given below is NOT logically possible, based on the given fact?

A. If (i), then (iv).
B. If (i), then (iii).
C. If (i), then (ii).
D. If (ii), then (iv).
questions.to_find: which conditional statement is NOT logically possible given the fact 'rain implies field is wet'

understand.plain: The given fact is a conditional: rain -> field is wet. We need to identify which of the four stated conditionals directly contradicts this fact. If it rains, the field must be wet; so a scenario where it rains AND the field is not wet is a direct logical contradiction.

keywords.explain: If P implies Q, and P is true, then Q must be true.
keywords.example: Rain implies wet field. It rained. Therefore the field is wet.

keywords.explain: If P implies Q, and Q is false, then P must be false.
keywords.example: Rain implies wet field. Field is not wet. Therefore it did not rain.

keywords.explain: A situation that violates a given fact or rule, making it impossible.
keywords.example: 'It rained AND the field is not wet' contradicts the given fact.

understand.visual_alt: Arrow diagram: rain -> wet field, with the four conditional statements as possible scenarios.

given.aim: Find the conditional among options A-D that cannot be true given the stated fact.

terms.meaning: Whenever it rains, the field is necessarily wet.
terms.example: P = 'It rains', Q = 'field is wet'. P -> Q is given as always true.
terms.connects: This fact constrains all possible true/false combinations of P and Q.

terms.term: Option A: If (i) then (iv) — 'It rains' implies 'It did not rain'
terms.meaning: Would mean: given it rains, it did not rain — a contradiction by itself, but this is asking if this CONDITIONAL is possible (it's odd but not a violation of the base fact by itself).
terms.example: Actually this conditional says rain -> ~rain, which forces rain to be false; that is consistent (rain just never occurs).
terms.connects: Not violating the rain->wet fact since rain is absent.

terms.term: Option C: If (i) then (ii) — 'It rains' implies 'field is not wet'
terms.meaning: Would mean: rain -> NOT wet field. This directly contradicts the given fact rain -> wet field.
terms.example: The given fact says rain makes the field wet; this option says rain makes the field not wet.
terms.connects: Directly opposes the base fact, making it logically impossible.

given.plan: Check each option against the base fact P -> Q. An option 'if X then Y' is NOT possible if having X true forces a conclusion about Q that contradicts P -> Q.

steps.title: State the base fact in formal logic
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: Base fact: rain -> wet_field. The only forbidden row is: rain = TRUE and wet_field = FALSE.
steps.note: All other combinations (no rain + dry, no rain + wet, rain + wet) are consistent with the base fact.

steps.title: Check Option A: If (i) then (iv) — rain -> NOT rain
steps.formula_raw: An implication with a False antecedent is automatically True
steps.apply: This conditional means: whenever it rains, conclude it did not rain. This forces the antecedent (rain) to always be false — meaning it simply never rains. That is logically consistent with the base fact (no rain means we never trigger rain -> wet). Option A is possible.
steps.note: Unusual but not contradictory to the base fact.

steps.title: Check Option B: If (i) then (iii) — rain -> field is wet
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
steps.apply: This is exactly the base fact itself: rain -> wet. This is directly supported. Option B is logically possible (and is the direct restatement of the fact).
steps.note: Option B is the most obviously possible — it restates the given fact.

steps.title: Check Option C: If (i) then (ii) — rain -> field is NOT wet
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: This says: rain -> NOT wet. But the base fact says: rain -> wet. If it rains, the field must be wet (base fact) AND must not be wet (option C). This is a direct contradiction. Option C is NOT logically possible.
steps.note: This is the correct answer — option C violates the given fact.

steps.title: Check Option D: If (ii) then (iv) — NOT wet -> NOT rain
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: The contrapositive of the base fact (rain -> wet) is: NOT wet -> NOT rain. Option D is the contrapositive of the base fact, which is always logically equivalent and thus always valid. Option D is logically necessary.
steps.note: Contrapositive is always valid alongside the original implication.

solution.result: Option C (If it rains, then the field is NOT wet) directly contradicts the base fact and is NOT logically possible. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The question tests knowledge of implication truth tables and the contrapositive law; all relevant formulas are in the registry.

questions.question:
Consider the following sentences:
All benches are beds.
No bed is a bulb.
Some bulbs are lamps.

Which of the following can be inferred?
i. Some beds are lamps.
ii. Some lamps are beds.

A. Only i
B. Only ii
C. Both i and ii
D. Neither i nor ii
questions.to_find: which of the two proposed inferences follows from the three given categorical statements about benches, beds, bulbs, and lamps

understand.plain: Three categorical statements define relationships among four categories. We need to trace whether any bed can be a lamp and whether any lamp can be a bed. The chain from beds to lamps requires going through bulbs, but beds and bulbs are entirely disjoint (no bed is a bulb), so the chain breaks.

keywords.explain: States that the sets A and B have no members in common; they are completely disjoint.
keywords.example: 'No bed is a bulb' means the intersection of beds and bulbs is empty.

keywords.explain: States that at least one member of A is also in B, but not necessarily all.
keywords.example: 'Some bulbs are lamps' means at least one bulb is also a lamp.

keywords.explain: A sequence of categorical propositions where the conclusion of one feeds into the next. The chain breaks if a universal negative severs a link.
keywords.example: All benches are beds -> No bed is a bulb => No bench is a bulb.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='240' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='10'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='120' rx='80' ry='55' class='hi'/><ellipse cx='155' cy='120' rx='55' ry='35' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' fill-opacity='0.4' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='320' cy='100' rx='75' ry='50' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='355' cy='115' rx='55' ry='38' fill='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' fill-opacity='0.25' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='245' y1='120' x2='255' y2='120' class='no' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='8,4'/><text x='110' y='90' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Beds</text><text x='145' y='128' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Benches</text><text x='315' y='78' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bulbs</text><text x='368' y='125' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Lamps</text><text x='248' y='145' class='tag no' text-anchor='middle'>Disjoint</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing beds (containing benches) completely separate from bulbs (partially overlapping lamps); the disjoint gap between beds and bulbs blocks any connection to lamps.

given.aim: Determine whether beds can be lamps or lamps can be beds based on the chain of categorical facts.

terms.meaning: Set of benches is a subset of set of beds.
terms.example: Every bench is also a bed.
terms.connects: Establishes benches as a subset within beds.

terms.meaning: The set of beds and the set of bulbs are completely disjoint; their intersection is empty.
terms.example: Not a single bed can be a bulb, and vice versa.
terms.connects: Creates a complete barrier between the beds-group and the bulbs-group.

terms.meaning: There is at least one bulb that is also a lamp, but lamps can also include non-bulbs.
terms.example: Some lamps are bulbs, but some lamps might not be bulbs.
terms.connects: Lamps overlap with bulbs, but the bed-bulb barrier means this overlap cannot touch beds.

given.plan: Trace the logical chain from beds to lamps. Since no bed is a bulb, and lamps only overlap with bulbs (not beds), no bed can be a lamp. Test the converse (some lamp is a bed) by the same barrier.

steps.title: Establish the full set relationships
steps.formula_raw: 'All A are B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Bench subset Bed (all benches are beds). Bed INTERSECT Bulb = empty (no bed is a bulb). Bulb INTERSECT Lamp != empty (some bulbs are lamps, so lamps and bulbs share members).
steps.note: The critical constraint is Bed INTERSECT Bulb = empty, which creates a total separation.

steps.title: Test Inference i: Some beds are lamps
steps.formula_raw: serial: forall x exists y R(x,y); irreflexive: forall x ~R(x,x)
steps.apply: For a bed to be a lamp it would need to be a bulb first (since lamps that we know of are bulbs). But no bed is a bulb. Could a lamp be not-a-bulb? The only information we have about lamps is that SOME bulbs are lamps; we have no information linking lamps to beds at all. We cannot infer any bed is a lamp. Inference i is FALSE.
steps.note: The absence of any bridge between beds and lamps means we cannot conclude any bed is a lamp.

steps.title: Test Inference ii: Some lamps are beds
steps.apply: Inference ii is the converse of inference i. If no bed is a lamp (which we established in step 2 by the beds-bulbs barrier), then certainly no lamp is a bed either. The relationship is symmetric for set membership: bed intersect lamp = empty implies lamp intersect bed = empty. Inference ii is also FALSE.
steps.note: Set intersection is symmetric; if Bed intersect Lamp = empty then Lamp intersect Bed = empty.

solution.result: Neither inference i nor inference ii can be logically deduced from the given statements. The beds-bulbs disjointness blocks any possible link between beds and lamps. Option D is correct.

questions.formula_note: The solution relies on set disjointness propagation through categorical syllogism; all referenced formulas are in the existing registry.
